question:
B-5. (a) Let \( z \) be a solution of the quadratic equation
\[
a z^{2}+b z+c=0
\]
and let \( n \) be a positive integer. Show that \( z \) can be expressed as a rational function of \( z^{n}, a, b, c \).
(b) Using (a) or by any other means, express \( x \) as a rational function of \( x^{3} \) and \( x+(1 / x) \). (Display your answer explicitly in a clearly visible form.)
[By a rational function of several variables, we mean a quotient of polynomials in those variables, the polynomials having rational numbers as coefficients, and the denominator being not identically zero. Thus to obtain \( x \) as a rational function of \( u=x^{2} \) and \( v= \) \( x+(1 / x) \), we could write \( x=(u+1) / v \).
solution:
B-5. (a) Let \( r=-b / a \) and \( s=-c / a \). Let polynomials \( p_{n} \) and \( q_{n} \) in \( r \) and \( s \) be defined by the initial conditions \( p_{0}=0, p_{1}=1, q_{0}=1 \), and \( q_{1}=0 \) and the recursion formulas \( p_{n}=r p_{n-1}+s p_{n-2} \) and \( q_{n}=r q_{n-1}+s q_{n-2} \) for \( n>1 \). Using \( z^{n}=r z^{n-1}+s z^{n-2} \) and mathematical induction, one proves that \( z^{n}=p_{n} z+q_{n} \) and that all the coefficients in \( p_{n}(r, s) \) are positive. Then multiplying numerator and denominator of the right hand side of \( z=\left[z^{n}-q_{n}(-b / a,-c / a)\right] / p_{n}(-b / a,-c / a) \) by the proper power of \( a \) leads to \( z=F\left(z^{n}, a, b, c\right) / G(a, b, c) \), where \( F \) and \( G \) are polynomials with integer coefficients. Since all the coefficients in \( p_{n}(r, s) \) are positive, the same is true of \( G(a, b, c) \). Therefore \( G(a, b, c) \) is not identically zero and \( F / G \) is the desired rational function.
(b) Let \( v=x+(1 / x) \). Then \( x^{2}-v x+1=0 \). Using (a) with \( z \) replaced by \( x \), one finds that \( x^{3}=p_{3} x+q_{3} \) with \( p_{3}=v^{2}-1 \) and \( q_{3}=-v \). Then
\[
x=\left(x^{3}-q_{3}\right) / p_{3}=\left(x^{3}+v\right) /\left(v^{2}-1\right) .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
B-5. (a) Let \( rootvariable \) be a solution of the quadratic equation
\[
coeffalpha rootvariable^{2}+coeffbeta rootvariable+coeffgamma=0
\]
and let \( positiveindex \) be a positive integer. Show that \( rootvariable \) can be expressed as a rational function of \( rootvariable^{positiveindex}, coeffalpha, coeffbeta, coeffgamma \).
(b) Using (a) or by any other means, express \( basevariable \) as a rational function of \( basevariable^{3} \) and \( basevariable+(1 / basevariable) \). (Display your answer explicitly in a clearly visible form.)
[By a rational function of several variables, we mean a quotient of polynomials in those variables, the polynomials having rational numbers as coefficients, and the denominator being not identically zero. Thus to obtain \( basevariable \) as a rational function of \( squarevalue=basevariable^{2} \) and \( suminverse= \) \( basevariable+(1 / basevariable) \), we could write \( basevariable=(squarevalue+1) / suminverse \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-5. (a) Let \( coeffrho=-coeffbeta / coeffalpha \) and \( coeffsigma=-coeffgamma / coeffalpha \). Let polynomials \( polyindex \) and \( quasiindex \) in \( coeffrho \) and \( coeffsigma \) be defined by the initial conditions \( polyzero=0, polyone=1, quasizero=1 \), and \( quasione=0 \) and the recursion formulas \( polyindex=coeffrho polypreindex+coeffsigma polypreprev \) and \( quasiindex=coeffrho quasipreindex+coeffsigma quasipreprev \) for \( positiveindex>1 \). Using \( rootvariable^{positiveindex}=coeffrho rootvariable^{positiveindex-1}+coeffsigma rootvariable^{positiveindex-2} \) and mathematical induction, one proves that \( rootvariable^{positiveindex}=polyindex rootvariable+quasiindex \) and that all the coefficients in \( polyindex(coeffrho, coeffsigma) \) are positive. Then multiplying numerator and denominator of the right hand side of \( rootvariable=\left[rootvariable^{positiveindex}-quasiindex(-coeffbeta / coeffalpha,-coeffgamma / coeffalpha)\right] / polyindex(-coeffbeta / coeffalpha,-coeffgamma / coeffalpha) \) by the proper power of \( coeffalpha \) leads to \( rootvariable=numerpoly\left(rootvariable^{positiveindex}, coeffalpha, coeffbeta, coeffgamma\right) / denompoly(coeffalpha, coeffbeta, coeffgamma) \), where numerpoly and denompoly are polynomials with integer coefficients. Since all the coefficients in \( polyindex(coeffrho, coeffsigma) \) are positive, the same is true of \( denompoly(coeffalpha, coeffbeta, coeffgamma) \). Therefore \( denompoly(coeffalpha, coeffbeta, coeffgamma) \) is not identically zero and \( numerpoly / denompoly \) is the desired rational function.
(b) Let \( suminverse=basevariable+(1 / basevariable) \). Then \( basevariable^{2}-suminverse basevariable+1=0 \). Using (a) with \( rootvariable \) replaced by \( basevariable \), one finds that \( basevariable^{3}=p_{3} basevariable+q_{3} \) with \( p_{3}=suminverse^{2}-1 \) and \( q_{3}=-suminverse \). Then
\[
basevariable=\left(basevariable^{3}+suminverse\right) /\left(suminverse^{2}-1\right) .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-5. (a) Let \( pineapple \) be a solution of the quadratic equation
\[
backpack\, pineapple^{2}+suitcase\, pineapple+zeppelin=0
\]
and let \( carousel \) be a positive integer. Show that \( pineapple \) can be expressed as a rational function of \( pineapple^{carousel}, backpack, suitcase, zeppelin \).
(b) Using (a) or by any other means, express \( hamburger \) as a rational function of \( hamburger^{3} \) and \( hamburger+(1 / hamburger) \). (Display your answer explicitly in a clearly visible form.)
[By a rational function of several variables, we mean a quotient of polynomials in those variables, the polynomials having rational numbers as coefficients, and the denominator being not identically zero. Thus to obtain \( hamburger \) as a rational function of \( telescope=hamburger^{2} \) and \( marigold= \) \( hamburger+(1 / hamburger) \), we could write \( hamburger=(telescope+1) / marigold \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-5. (a) Let \( dinosaur=-suitcase / backpack \) and \( chocolate=-zeppelin / backpack \). Let polynomials \( watermelon \) and \( candlelight \) in \( dinosaur \) and \( chocolate \) be defined by the initial conditions \( lighthouse=0, snowflake=1, guitarist=1 \), and \( astronaut=0 \) and the recursion formulas \( watermelon=dinosaur butterfly+chocolate landscape \) and \( candlelight=dinosaur helicopter+chocolate pomegranate \) for \( carousel>1 \). Using \( pineapple^{carousel}=dinosaur pineapple^{carousel-1}+chocolate pineapple^{carousel-2} \) and mathematical induction, one proves that \( pineapple^{carousel}=watermelon pineapple+candlelight \) and that all the coefficients in \( watermelon(dinosaur, chocolate) \) are positive. Then multiplying numerator and denominator of the right hand side of \( pineapple=\left[pineapple^{carousel}-candlelight(-suitcase / backpack,-zeppelin / backpack)\right] / watermelon(-suitcase / backpack,-zeppelin / backpack) \) by the proper power of \( backpack \) leads to \( pineapple=barnstorm\left(pineapple^{carousel}, backpack, suitcase, zeppelin\right) / horseshoe(backpack, suitcase, zeppelin) \), where \( barnstorm \) and \( horseshoe \) are polynomials with integer coefficients. Since all the coefficients in \( watermelon(dinosaur, chocolate) \) are positive, the same is true of \( horseshoe(backpack, suitcase, zeppelin) \). Therefore \( horseshoe(backpack, suitcase, zeppelin) \) is not identically zero and \( barnstorm / horseshoe \) is the desired rational function.
(b) Let \( marigold=hamburger+(1 / hamburger) \). Then \( hamburger^{2}-marigold\, hamburger+1=0 \). Using (a) with \( pineapple \) replaced by \( hamburger \), one finds that \( hamburger^{3}=p_{3} hamburger+q_{3} \) with \( p_{3}=marigold^{2}-1 \) and \( q_{3}=-marigold \). Then
\[
hamburger=\left(hamburger^{3}-q_{3}\right) / p_{3}=\left(hamburger^{3}+marigold\right) /\left(marigold^{2}-1\right) .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-5. (a) Let \( summitpoint \) be a solution of the quadratic equation
\[
trailing \, summitpoint^{2}+outercoef \, summitpoint+firstcoef=0
\]
and let \( negative \) be a positive integer. Show that \( summitpoint \) can be expressed as a rational function of \( summitpoint^{negative}, trailing, outercoef, firstcoef \).
(b) Using (a) or by any other means, express \( knownvar \) as a rational function of \( knownvar^{3} \) and \( knownvar+(1 / knownvar) \). (Display your answer explicitly in a clearly visible form.)
[By a rational function of several variables, we mean a quotient of polynomials in those variables, the polynomials having rational numbers as coefficients, and the denominator being not identically zero. Thus to obtain \( knownvar \) as a rational function of \( rootvalue=knownvar^{2} \) and \( difference= \) \( knownvar+(1 / knownvar) \), we could write \( knownvar=(rootvalue+1) / difference \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-5. (a) Let \( productor=-outercoef / trailing \) and \( collective=-firstcoef / trailing \). Let polynomials \( genericpoly \) and \( genericcoef \) in \( productor \) and \( collective \) be defined by the initial conditions \( finalpoly=0, initialpoly=1, finalcoef=1 \), and \( initialcoef=0 \) and the recursion formulas \( genericpoly=productor previouspoly+collective preprevpoly \) and \( genericcoef=productor previouscoef+collective preprevcoef \) for \( negative>1 \). Using \( summitpoint^{negative}=productor summitpoint^{negative-1}+collective summitpoint^{negative-2} \) and mathematical induction, one proves that \( summitpoint^{negative}=genericpoly summitpoint+genericcoef \) and that all the coefficients in \( genericpoly(productor, collective) \) are positive. Then multiplying numerator and denominator of the right hand side of \( summitpoint=\left[summitpoint^{negative}-genericcoef(-outercoef / trailing,-firstcoef / trailing)\right] / genericpoly(-outercoef / trailing,-firstcoef / trailing) \) by the proper power of \( trailing \) leads to \( summitpoint=denominator\left(summitpoint^{negative}, trailing, outercoef, firstcoef\right) / numerator(trailing, outercoef, firstcoef) \), where \( denominator \) and \( numerator \) are polynomials with integer coefficients. Since all the coefficients in \( genericpoly(productor, collective) \) are positive, the same is true of \( numerator(trailing, outercoef, firstcoef) \). Therefore \( numerator(trailing, outercoef, firstcoef) \) is not identically zero and \( denominator / numerator \) is the desired rational function.
(b) Let \( difference=knownvar+(1 / knownvar) \). Then \( knownvar^{2}-difference knownvar+1=0 \). Using (a) with \( summitpoint \) replaced by \( knownvar \), one finds that \( knownvar^{3}=genericpoly knownvar+genericcoef \) with \( genericpoly=difference^{2}-1 \) and \( genericcoef=-difference \). Then
\[
knownvar=\left(knownvar^{3}-genericcoef\right) / genericpoly=\left(knownvar^{3}+difference\right) /\left(difference^{2}-1\right) .
\]

garbled_string.question:
B-5. (a) Let \( qzxwvtnp \) be a solution of the quadratic equation
\[
kfwlanop qzxwvtnp^{2}+cszmjdqe qzxwvtnp+ylgtbvio=0
\]
and let \( vfglimqu \) be a positive integer. Show that \( qzxwvtnp \) can be expressed as a rational function of \( qzxwvtnp^{vfglimqu}, kfwlanop, cszmjdqe, ylgtbvio \).
(b) Using (a) or by any other means, express \( hjgrksla \) as a rational function of \( hjgrksla^{3} \) and \( hjgrksla+(1 / hjgrksla) \). (Display your answer explicitly in a clearly visible form.)
[By a rational function of several variables, we mean a quotient of polynomials in those variables, the polynomials having rational numbers as coefficients, and the denominator being not identically zero. Thus to obtain \( hjgrksla \) as a rational function of \( tbskdnea=hjgrksla^{2} \) and \( pxjrmhao= hjgrksla+(1 / hjgrksla) \), we could write \( hjgrksla=(tbskdnea+1) / pxjrmhao \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-5. (a) Let \( hqzfldwa=-cszmjdqe / kfwlanop \) and \( wepqmxor=-ylgtbvio / kfwlanop \). Let polynomials \( jxrhabks \) and \( twlmpsha \) in \( hqzfldwa \) and \( wepqmxor \) be defined by the initial conditions \( lidwqnez=0, gpyfkrso=1, fakmesot=1 \), and \( ziqbrduy=0 \) and the recursion formulas \( jxrhabks=hqzfldwa\,mcltvezp+wepqmxor\,dwrqkgvz \) and \( twlmpsha=hqzfldwa\,bsgwxyif+wepqmxor\,uhvozrpa \) for \( vfglimqu>1 \). Using \( qzxwvtnp^{vfglimqu}=hqzfldwa\,qzxwvtnp^{vfglimqu-1}+wepqmxor\,qzxwvtnp^{vfglimqu-2} \) and mathematical induction, one proves that \( qzxwvtnp^{vfglimqu}=jxrhabks\,qzxwvtnp+twlmpsha \) and that all the coefficients in \( jxrhabks(hqzfldwa, wepqmxor) \) are positive. Then multiplying numerator and denominator of the right hand side of 
\[
qzxwvtnp=\left[qzxwvtnp^{vfglimqu}-twlmpsha(-cszmjdqe / kfwlanop,-ylgtbvio / kfwlanop)\right]\big/ jxrhabks(-cszmjdqe / kfwlanop,-ylgtbvio / kfwlanop)
\]
by the proper power of \( kfwlanop \) leads to 
\[
qzxwvtnp=neuxgkdc\left(qzxwvtnp^{vfglimqu}, kfwlanop, cszmjdqe, ylgtbvio\right) / rovjtdal(kfwlanop, cszmjdqe, ylgtbvio),
\]
where \( neuxgkdc \) and \( rovjtdal \) are polynomials with integer coefficients. Since all the coefficients in \( jxrhabks(hqzfldwa, wepqmxor) \) are positive, the same is true of \( rovjtdal(kfwlanop, cszmjdqe, ylgtbvio) \). Therefore \( rovjtdal(kfwlanop, cszmjdqe, ylgtbvio) \) is not identically zero and \( neuxgkdc / rovjtdal \) is the desired rational function.
(b) Let \( pxjrmhao=hjgrksla+(1 / hjgrksla) \). Then \( hjgrksla^{2}-pxjrmhao\,hjgrksla+1=0 \). Using (a) with \( qzxwvtnp \) replaced by \( hjgrksla \), one finds that \( hjgrksla^{3}=p_{3}\,hjgrksla+q_{3} \) with \( p_{3}=pxjrmhao^{2}-1 \) and \( q_{3}=-pxjrmhao \). Then
\[
hjgrksla=\left(hjgrksla^{3}-q_{3}\right) / p_{3}=\left(hjgrksla^{3}+pxjrmhao\right) /\left(pxjrmhao^{2}-1\right) .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
(a)   Let  
\[
a z^{3}+b z^{2}+c z+d=0\qquad\qquad (1)
\]  
be a cubic with rational coefficients and assume  
\[
a\neq 0,\qquad d\neq 0 ,\qquad   
\Delta:=b^{2}c^{2}-4ac^{3}-4b^{3}d-27a^{2}d^{2}+18a b c d\neq 0 .
\]  
Introduce the reduced parameters  
\[
p=-\dfrac{b}{a},\qquad q=-\dfrac{c}{a},\qquad r=-\dfrac{d}{a}. \tag{2}
\]

Fix an integer $n\ge 2$.

(i)  Show that there exist unique polynomials  
\[
P_{n}(p,q,r),\;B_{n}(p,q,r),\;C_{n}(p,q,r)\in\mathbb{Q}[p,q,r]\tag{3}
\]  
such that  
\[
z^{n}=P_{n}+B_{n}z+C_{n}z^{2}. \tag{4}
\]

(ii)  Put  
\[
K_{n}:=\mathbb{Q}(a,b,c,d,z^{n})
\]
and consider over $K_{n}$ the quadratic polynomial
\[
Q_{n}(T)=C_{n}\,T^{2}+B_{n}\,T+\bigl(P_{n}-z^{n}\bigr). \tag{5}
\]

1.  Prove that $Q_{n}(z)=0$ and deduce

   *  if $(B_{n},C_{n})=(0,0)$ then $z^{n}\in\mathbb{Q}(a,b,c,d)$; hence  
     $K_{n}=\mathbb{Q}(a,b,c,d)$ and  
     \[
       [K_{n}(z):K_{n}]=\deg_{\mathbb{Q}}(z)\in\{1,2,3\},
     \]  
     the value $3$ occurring precisely when the cubic $(1)$ is
     irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$;

   *  if $(B_{n},C_{n})\neq(0,0)$ then $[K_{n}(z):K_{n}]\le 2$.

   Treat separately the sub-cases  

   - $C_{n}=0,\;B_{n}\neq 0$ (linear case),  

   - $C_{n}\neq 0$ (proper quadratic).

   Show moreover that for an irreducible cubic $(1)$ one has  
   \[
   (B_{n},C_{n})=(0,0)
   \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
   p=q=0\text{ and }3\mid n. \tag{6}
   \]

2.  Assuming $C_{n}\neq 0$, derive the quadratic-formula identity  
\[
z=\dfrac{-B_{n}\pm\sqrt{\Delta_{n}}}{2C_{n}},\qquad 
\Delta_{n}=B_{n}^{2}+4C_{n}\bigl(z^{n}-P_{n}\bigr)\in K_{n}. \tag{7}
\]

3.  Assume now that  

   *  the cubic $(1)$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$;  
   *  its discriminant $\Delta$ is not a square in $\mathbb{Q}$.

   Let $z_{1}=z,z_{2},z_{3}$ be the roots of $(1)$ and define  
   \[
   \mu:=\dfrac{z_{2}}{z_{1}},\qquad 
   \nu:=\dfrac{z_{3}}{z_{1}},\qquad 
   R:=\{1,\mu,\nu,\mu^{-1},\nu^{-1},\mu/\nu\}. \tag{8}
   \]

   (a)  Prove that  
        \[
        [\mathbb{Q}(\mu):\mathbb{Q}]
        =[\,\mathbb{Q}(\nu):\mathbb{Q}\,]\in\{2,3,6\}.
        \]
        Show further that if one of $\mu,\nu$ is a root of unity, then its
        order lies in $\{1,2,3,4,6\}$.

   (b)  Put $L$ for the splitting field of $(1)$ and
        \[
          H_{n}:=\{\tau\in\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\mid
          (\tau(z)/z)^{\,n}=1\}.
        \]
        Establish the chain of equivalences  
        \[
        \bigl[(B_{n},C_{n})\neq(0,0)\bigr]\;\wedge\;
        \Bigl(Q_{n}\text{ irreducible over }K_{n}\Bigr)
        \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
        z\notin K_{n}
        \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
        R\setminus\{1\}\text{ contains an }n\text{-th root of unity}. \tag{9}
        \]

   (c)  Deduce:

        *  If no element of $R\setminus\{1\}$ is a root of unity, then
          $(B_{n},C_{n})\neq(0,0)$ and $Q_{n}(T)$ splits over $K_{n}$,
          so $z\in K_{n}$ for every $n\ge 2$.

        *  If the subgroup $\langle\mu,\nu\rangle\subset L^{\times}$ is
          finite of order $m\in\{2,3,4,6\}$, then, for all $n\ge 2$,
          \[
          Q_{n}(T)\text{ is irreducible over }K_{n}
          \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
          \gcd(m,n)\neq 1,
          \]
          whereas $Q_{n}(T)$ splits (and therefore $z\in K_{n}$)
          when $\gcd(m,n)=1$.

(b)  For every non-zero complex number $x$ put  
\[
u=x+\dfrac{1}{x}.
\]
Prove the inversion identity  
\[
x=\dfrac{x^{7}+u^{5}-4u^{3}+3u}{\,u^{6}-5u^{4}+6u^{2}-1\,} \tag{10}
\]
and verify that the denominator is not the zero polynomial, so the
formula is valid for all $x$ satisfying
$u^{6}-5u^{4}+6u^{2}-1\neq 0$.


%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
(a)

(i)  \emph{Existence and uniqueness of the reduction polynomials.}  
Dividing $(1)$ by $a$ gives the monic relation  
\[
z^{3}=p z^{2}+q z+r. \tag{11}
\]
Put  
\[
\mathcal{V}:=\operatorname{Span}_{\mathbb{Q}}\{\,1,z,z^{2}\,\}.
\]
Because any power $z^{k}\;(k\ge 3)$ can be reduced with $(11)$ to an
element of $\mathcal{V}$, there exist polynomials
$P_{k},B_{k},C_{k}\in\mathbb{Q}[p,q,r]$ such that
\[
z^{k}=P_{k}+B_{k}z+C_{k}z^{2}\qquad(k\ge 0). \tag{12}
\]

Work in the fraction field $\mathbb{Q}(p,q,r)$.
Relation $(11)$ is the \emph{generic} monic cubic and is irreducible
over this field; consequently $1,z,z^{2}$ are linearly independent
over $\mathbb{Q}(p,q,r)$.
Hence representation $(12)$ is unique, so the reduction polynomials in
$(4)$ are well defined.

A convenient way to compute them is the matrix recurrence  
\[
\mathbf{v}_{k}:=\begin{pmatrix}P_{k}\\[2pt]B_{k}\\[2pt]C_{k}\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\mathbf{v}_{k+3}=p\,\mathbf{v}_{k+2}+q\,\mathbf{v}_{k+1}+r\,\mathbf{v}_{k},
\tag{13}
\]
started from  
$\mathbf{v}_{0}=(1,0,0)^{\mathrm T},\;
  \mathbf{v}_{1}=(0,1,0)^{\mathrm T},\;
  \mathbf{v}_{2}=(0,0,1)^{\mathrm T}$.

A special phenomenon occurs when $p=q=0$: then $z^{3}=r$ and one
checks immediately from $(13)$ that  
\[
(B_{n},C_{n})=(0,0)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 3\mid n. \tag{14}
\]

%---------------------------------------------------------------------------
(ii-1)  Substituting $T=z$ in $Q_{n}(T)$ gives $Q_{n}(z)=0$.

\emph{Case $(B_{n},C_{n})=(0,0)$.}  
Then $z^{n}=P_{n}\in\mathbb{Q}(a,b,c,d)$, so
$K_{n}=\mathbb{Q}(a,b,c,d)$ and consequently  
\[
[K_{n}(z):K_{n}]=\deg_{\mathbb{Q}}(z)\in\{1,2,3\},
\]
the value $3$ occurring exactly when the cubic $(1)$ is irreducible
over $\mathbb{Q}$.

\emph{Case $(B_{n},C_{n})\neq(0,0)$.}

* If $C_{n}=0$ but $B_{n}\neq 0$, then  
\[
z=\dfrac{z^{n}-P_{n}}{B_{n}}\in K_{n},
\]
so $[K_{n}(z):K_{n}]=1\le 2$.

* If $C_{n}\neq 0$, then $Q_{n}(T)$ is a genuine quadratic
with root $z$; hence $[K_{n}(z):K_{n}]\le 2$.

%---------------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{The equivalence $(6)$ for irreducible cubics - corrected proof.}

Assume from now on that the cubic $(1)$ is \emph{irreducible} over
$\mathbb{Q}$.
Let $L$ be its splitting field.  
Because $\Delta\notin\mathbb{Q}^{2}$, one has  
\[
\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\;\cong\;S_{3}. \tag{15}
\]
Choose the $3$-cycle  
\[
\sigma:\; z_{1}\mapsto z_{2},\; z_{2}\mapsto z_{3},\; z_{3}\mapsto z_{1},
\qquad z_{1}=z. \tag{16}
\]
Put  
\[
\mu:=\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}},\qquad
\nu:=\frac{z_{3}}{z_{1}}. \tag{17}
\]

\subsubsection*{Step A:  $(B_{n},C_{n})=(0,0)\;\Longrightarrow\;\mu^{\,n}=1$.}

Indeed, $(B_{n},C_{n})=(0,0)$ means $z^{n}\in\mathbb{Q}$.  
Applying $\sigma$ we obtain
\[
z^{\,n}=\sigma\bigl(z^{\,n}\bigr)=z_{2}^{\,n}
        =(\mu z)^{\,n}=\mu^{\,n}z^{\,n},
\]
hence $\mu^{\,n}=1$.
An identical argument with $\sigma^{2}$ gives $\nu^{\,n}=1$.

\subsubsection*{Step B:  If two distinct roots differ by a root of unity,
then that root of unity is \emph{necessarily} a primitive third root and
$p=q=0$.}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:new}
Let $f(X)=X^{3}+bX^{2}+cX+d\in\mathbb{Q}[X]$ be irreducible and let
$z_{1},z_{2}$ be two distinct roots with
$z_{2}=\zeta\,z_{1}$ for some root of unity $\zeta\neq 1$.
Then $\zeta^{3}=1$ and $b=c=0$.
\end{lemma}

\emph{Proof.}
Define
\[
g(X):=f(X)-f(\zeta X)
          =(1-\zeta^{3})X^{3}+b(1-\zeta^{2})X^{2}+c(1-\zeta)X. \tag{18}
\]
Because $f(z_{1})=f(z_{2})=0$, the number $z_{1}$ is a common root of
$f$ and $g$.

$\bullet$ If $\zeta^{3}\neq 1$, then the coefficient $1-\zeta^{3}$ is
non-zero, so $\deg g=3$ and $g$ is \emph{not} a scalar multiple of $f$
(their coefficients of $X^{3}$ differ).
Hence $\gcd(f,g)$ is a non-constant proper divisor of $f$, contradicting
the irreducibility of $f$.
Therefore we must have $\zeta^{3}=1$.

$\bullet$ Put now $\zeta^{3}=1$ with $\zeta\neq 1$ (so $\zeta$ is a
primitive third root of unity).
Then $g(X)=b(1-\zeta^{2})X^{2}+c(1-\zeta)X$ has degree at most $2$ and
still vanishes at $z_{1}$.
If at least one of $b$ or $c$ is non-zero, the degree of $g$ is
$1$ or $2$ and $\gcd(f,g)$ is again a proper non-constant factor of
$f$, impossible.
Consequently $b=c=0$, i.e.\ $p=q=0$. \qed

\smallskip
Lemma \ref{lem:new} shows that if $\mu$ (or $\nu$) is a root of unity,
then $\mu^{3}=1$ and $p=q=0$.

\subsubsection*{Step C:  Completion of $(6)$.}

Assume $(B_{n},C_{n})=(0,0)$.  
Step A gives $\mu^{\,n}=1$; if $\mu\neq 1$, Lemma \ref{lem:new} yields
$\mu^{3}=1$ and $p=q=0$.  
Because $\mu^{\,3}=1$, necessarily $3\mid n$.
If $\mu=1$ then $z_{1}=z_{2}$, impossible; hence $\mu\neq 1$ and the
previous conclusion applies.

Conversely, if $p=q=0$ then $z^{3}=r$ and relation $(14)$ shows that
$(B_{n},C_{n})=(0,0)$ exactly when $3\mid n$.
Thus
\[
(B_{n},C_{n})=(0,0)
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
p=q=0\text{ and }3\mid n,
\]
establishing $(6)$.

%---------------------------------------------------------------------------
(ii-2)  When $C_{n}\neq 0$, the quadratic formula applied to
$Q_{n}(T)=0$ yields $(7)$.

%---------------------------------------------------------------------------
(ii-3)  Assume now $(1)$ irreducible and $\Delta\notin\mathbb{Q}^{2}$.
We work with the notations in $(8)$.

(\alpha )  The stabiliser of $\mu$ (or $\nu$) in $S_{3}$ can have order
$1,2$ or $3$; its index is therefore $6,3$ or $2$, giving
$[\mathbb{Q}(\mu):\mathbb{Q}]\in\{6,3,2\}$.
A root of unity of degree at most $6$ has order in
$\{1,2,3,4,6\}$, establishing the first assertions.

(\beta )  For $\tau\in\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})$ set  
\[
\rho(\tau):=\dfrac{\tau(z)}{z}\in R .  \tag{19}
\]
Because $\tau(z^{\,n})=\rho(\tau)^{\,n}z^{\,n}$,
\[
H_{n}=\bigl\{\tau\in\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\mid
        \rho(\tau)^{\,n}=1\bigr\}. \tag{20}
\]

(\gamma )  If $(B_{n},C_{n})\neq(0,0)$,
then $Q_{n}$ splits over $K_{n}$ precisely when $z\in K_{n}$,
and is irreducible otherwise.
Using $(20)$ one obtains
\[
Q_{n}(T)\text{ irreducible over }K_{n}
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\exists\tau\in S_{3}:\rho(\tau)\neq 1,\; \rho(\tau)^{\,n}=1
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
R\setminus\{1\}\text{ contains an }n\text{-th root of unity}, \tag{21}
\]
which is the second equivalence in $(9)$.
Together with the cases distinguished at the beginning of (ii-1), this
gives the whole chain $(9)$.

(\delta )  The assertions in part (c) follow immediately from $(21)$.

%---------------------------------------------------------------------------
(b)  

From $u=x+\dfrac{1}{x}$ one has  
\[
x^{2}=u\,x-1. \tag{22}
\]
Repeatedly multiply by $x$ and reduce with $(22)$:
\[
\begin{aligned}
x^{3}&=(u^{2}-1)x-u,\\
x^{4}&=(u^{3}-2u)x-(u^{2}-1),\\
x^{5}&=(u^{4}-3u^{2}+1)x-u(u^{2}-2),\\
x^{6}&=(u^{5}-4u^{3}+3u)x-(u^{4}-3u^{2}+1),\\
x^{7}&=(u^{6}-5u^{4}+6u^{2}-1)x-(u^{5}-4u^{3}+3u). \tag{23}
\end{aligned}
\]
Solving the last line of $(23)$ for $x$ gives exactly the inversion
identity $(10)$.
The denominator $u^{6}-5u^{4}+6u^{2}-1$ is a non-zero polynomial, so
the identity is valid whenever this denominator does not vanish.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  The original problem dealt with a quadratic and used a single power zⁿ.
   Here we move to a *cubic*, which raises the ambient field degree from 2
   to 3 and forces one to manipulate *three* coefficient–dependent sequences
   simultaneously instead of one.  
•  Part (a) demands an explicit construction of Pₙ,Bₙ,Cₙ, the derivation of a
   pair of coupled linear equations, and the use of Cramer’s rule; none of
   these is needed for the quadratic case.  The proof also has to guarantee
   that some small n works for *all* cubics, invoking a non–trivial argument
   on the coefficients.  
•  Part (b) asks for the recovery of x from (x⁵,u).  The exponent 5 forces
   three rounds of substitutions (compare the single round required when the
   given power is x⁴ in the current kernel variant), and the resulting
   polynomials are of much higher degree (degree 4 in u).  Careful algebra is
   indispensable to avoid sign and coefficient errors.  
•  Together, the two parts blend linear–recurrence theory, determinant
   techniques, and clever algebraic manipulation, going noticeably beyond the
   elementary pattern-matching that suffices for the quadratic prototype.

original_kernel_variant.question:
(a)  Let  
\[
a z^{3}+b z^{2}+c z+d=0 \qquad\qquad (1)
\]  
be a cubic with rational coefficients and assume  
\[
a d\neq 0 ,\qquad   
\Delta:=b^{2}c^{2}-4ac^{3}-4b^{3}d-27a^{2}d^{2}+18a b c d\neq 0 .
\]  
Introduce  
\[
p=-\dfrac{b}{a},\qquad q=-\dfrac{c}{a},\qquad r=-\dfrac{d}{a}. \tag{2}
\]

Fix an integer \(n\ge 2\).

(i)  Prove that for every \(n\) there exist polynomials  
\[
P_{n}(p,q,r),\;B_{n}(p,q,r),\;C_{n}(p,q,r)\in\mathbb{Q}[p,q,r] \tag{3}
\]  
satisfying  
\[
z^{n}=P_{n}+B_{n}\,z+C_{n}\,z^{2}. \tag{4}
\]

Show further that these three polynomials are unique provided that the cubic \((1)\) is {\it irreducible} over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

(ii)  Put  
\[
K_{n}:=\mathbb{Q}(a,b,c,d,z^{n})
\]  
and consider the quadratic over \(K_{n}\)
\[
Q_{n}(T)=C_{n}\,T^{2}+B_{n}\,T+\bigl(P_{n}-z^{n}\bigr). \tag{5}
\]

1.  Prove that \(Q_{n}(z)=0\) and deduce \([K_{n}(z):K_{n}]\le 2\).
    (Explain separately why the statement is immediate when \(C_{n}=0\).)

2.  Assuming \(C_{n}\neq 0\), derive the quadratic-formula identity  
\[
z=\dfrac{-B_{n}\pm\sqrt{\Delta_{n}}}{2C_{n}},\qquad 
\Delta_{n}=B_{n}^{2}+4C_{n}(z^{n}-P_{n})\in K_{n}. \tag{6}
\]

3.  Assume from now on that  

   *  the cubic \((1)\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\);  
   *  its discriminant \(\Delta\) is not a square in \(\mathbb{Q}\)
     (equivalently, the splitting-field Galois group is \(S_{3}\)).

   Let \(z_{1}=z,z_{2},z_{3}\) be the roots of \((1)\) and define  
   \[
   \mu:=\dfrac{z_{2}}{z_{1}},\qquad 
   \nu:=\dfrac{z_{3}}{z_{1}},\qquad 
   R:=\{1,\mu,\nu,\mu^{-1},\nu^{-1},\mu/\nu\}. \tag{7}
   \]

   (a)  Prove that  
        \[
        [\mathbb{Q}(\mu):\mathbb{Q}]=[\mathbb{Q}(\nu):\mathbb{Q}]\in\{2,3,6\}.
        \]
        Show further that if one of \(\mu,\nu\) is a root of unity, then its
        order belongs to the set \(\{1,2,3,4,6\}\).

   (b)  For
        \[
          H_{n}:=\{\tau\in\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\mid
          (\tau(z)/z)^{\,n}=1\}
        \]
        prove the chain of equivalences  
        \[
        Q_{n}(T)\text{ is irreducible over }K_{n}
        \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
        z\notin K_{n}
        \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
        R\setminus\{1\}\text{ contains an }n\text{-th root of unity}. \tag{8}
        \]

   (c)  Deduce:

        *  If no element of \(R\setminus\{1\}\) is a root of unity, then
          \(Q_{n}(T)\) splits over \(K_{n}\) and \(z\in K_{n}\) for every
          \(n\ge 2\).

        *  If the subgroup \(\langle\mu,\nu\rangle\subset L^{\times}\) is
          finite of order \(m\in\{2,3,4,6\}\), then \(Q_{n}(T)\) is irreducible
          over \(K_{n}\) exactly for those integers \(n\) that share a common
          divisor with \(m\) (equivalently, for which \(\gcd(m,n)\neq1\)),
          and it splits otherwise.  Consequently \(z\) can be expressed as a
          rational function in \(z^{n}\) and \(a,b,c,d\) precisely when
          \(\gcd(m,n)=1\).

(b)  For every non-zero complex number \(x\) put  
\[
u=x+\dfrac{1}{x}.
\]
Prove the inversion identity  
\[
x=\dfrac{x^{7}+u^{5}-4u^{3}+3u}{\,u^{6}-5u^{4}+6u^{2}-1\,} \tag{9}
\]
and verify that the denominator is not the zero polynomial, so the
formula is valid for all \(x\) with
\(u^{6}-5u^{4}+6u^{2}-1\neq 0\).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
(a)  

(i)  Divide \((1)\) by \(a\) to obtain the monic relation  
\[
z^{3}=p z^{2}+q z+r. \tag{10}
\]
Put  
\[
\mathcal{V}:=\operatorname{Span}_{\mathbb{Q}}\{1,z,z^{2}\}.
\]
Because \((10)\) reduces every power \(z^{k}\;(k\ge 3)\) to an element of
\(\mathcal{V}\), there certainly exist polynomials
\(P_{n},B_{n},C_{n}\in\mathbb{Q}[p,q,r]\) satisfying \((4)\).

Assume now that the cubic \((1)\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\); then
\(\deg_{\mathbb{Q}}(z)=3\) and the family \(\{1,z,z^{2}\}\) is
\(\mathbb{Q}\)-linearly independent.  Hence the representation in \((4)\)
is unique: if  
\(P_{n}+B_{n}z+C_{n}z^{2}=P_{n}^{\prime}+B_{n}^{\prime}z+
C_{n}^{\prime}z^{2}\),  
then \((P_{n}-P_{n}^{\prime})+(B_{n}-B_{n}^{\prime})z+
(C_{n}-C_{n}^{\prime})z^{2}=0\) and independence forces
\(P_{n}=P_{n}^{\prime},\,B_{n}=B_{n}^{\prime},\,C_{n}=C_{n}^{\prime}\).

To compute these polynomials one may proceed inductively.  Assume  
\[
z^{k}=P_{k}+B_{k}z+C_{k}z^{2},\qquad k\ge 0. \tag{11}
\]
Multiplying by \(z\) and reducing the third power with \((10)\) shows  
\[
z^{k+3}=p z^{k+2}+q z^{k+1}+r z^{k}. \tag{12}
\]
Taking coefficients of \(1,z,z^{2}\) we obtain  
\[
\begin{pmatrix}P_{k+3}\\[2pt] B_{k+3}\\[2pt] C_{k+3}\end{pmatrix}
=
p\begin{pmatrix}P_{k+2}\\ B_{k+2}\\ C_{k+2}\end{pmatrix}
+
q\begin{pmatrix}P_{k+1}\\ B_{k+1}\\ C_{k+1}\end{pmatrix}
+
r\begin{pmatrix}P_{k}\\ B_{k}\\ C_{k}\end{pmatrix}. \tag{13}
\]
With the initial triples \((1,0,0),(0,1,0),(0,0,1)\) this triangular
recurrence determines uniquely the three sequences
\((P_{k})_{k\ge 0},(B_{k})_{k\ge 0},(C_{k})_{k\ge 0}\) in
\(\mathbb{Q}[p,q,r]\).  Setting \(k=n\) gives \((4)\).

%---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
(ii-1)  Substituting \(T=z\) in \(Q_{n}(T)\) shows \(Q_{n}(z)=0\), so
\([K_{n}(z):K_{n}]\le 2\).

If \(C_{n}=0\) then \(Q_{n}(T)\) is linear and yields
\[
z=\dfrac{z^{\,n}-P_{n}}{B_{n}}\in K_{n},
\]
hence \([K_{n}(z):K_{n}]=1\).  From now on we treat the generic case
\(C_{n}\neq 0\).

(ii-2)  When \(C_{n}\neq 0\) the quadratic formula applied to
\(Q_{n}(T)=0\) gives \((6)\).

%---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
(ii-3)  Throughout the remainder of part (a) we assume that the cubic
\((1)\) is irreducible and that \(\Delta\notin\mathbb{Q}^{2}\); hence the
splitting field \(L\) of \((1)\) satisfies
\(\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_{3}\).

Write \(z_{1}=z,z_{2},z_{3}\) for the three roots and define
\(\mu,\nu,R\) as in \((7)\).

(\alpha )  {\it Degrees of \(\mu,\nu\) and possible orders.}  
Because \(\mu,\nu\in L\), their degrees divide
\(|\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})|=6\).  The stabiliser of \(\mu\) in
\(S_{3}\) is one of
\(\{1\},\langle(1\,2)\rangle,\langle(1\,2\,3)\rangle\),
having indices \(6,3,2\) respectively, so
\([\mathbb{Q}(\mu):\mathbb{Q}]\in\{6,3,2\}\); the same holds for
\(\nu\).  Concrete examples such as
\(x^{3}-x+1,\;x^{3}-3x+1,\;x^{3}-3x^{2}+1\) exhibit the three
possibilities.

Assume \(\mu\) (or \(\nu\)) is a root of unity of order \(m\).
Then \(\mathbb{Q}(\mu)\subseteq\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{m})\) and so
\(\varphi(m)=[\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{m}):\mathbb{Q}]\) divides \(6\).
Besides the values \(1,2,3,4,6\) the divisibility \(\varphi(m)\mid6\)
also allows \(m=7,9,14,18\).  The cyclotomic fields of degree \(6\) are
Galois and have cyclic Galois groups, which cannot be embedded into
the non-abelian group \(S_{3}\); hence these four possibilities are
impossible, and the claimed list of orders follows.

(\beta )  {\it The subgroup fixing \(z^{n}\).}  
For \(\tau\in\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\) put  
\[
\rho(\tau):=\dfrac{\tau(z)}{z}\in R .  \tag{14}
\]
Because \(\tau(z^{\,n})=\rho(\tau)^{\,n}z^{\,n}\),  
\[
H_{n}=\{\tau\in \operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\mid
       \rho(\tau)^{\,n}=1\}. \tag{15}
\]

(\gamma )  {\it Irreducibility of \(Q_{n}(T)\).}  
Since \(Q_{n}(z)=0\),
\[
Q_{n}\text{ splits over }K_{n}\;\Longleftrightarrow\;z\in K_{n}.
\]
If \(z\notin K_{n}\) then \([K_{n}(z):K_{n}]=2\) and \(Q_{n}(T)\) is
irreducible.  Relation \((15)\) yields  
\[
Q_{n}(T)\text{ irreducible over }K_{n}
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\exists\tau\in S_{3}\text{ with }\rho(\tau)\neq1,\;
\rho(\tau)^{\,n}=1
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
R\setminus\{1\}\text{ contains an }n\text{-th root of unity}, \tag{16}
\]
which is precisely the chain of equivalences \((8)\).

(\delta )  {\it Consequences.}

*  If none of the five non-trivial elements of \(R\) is a root of unity,
  then condition \((16)\) never occurs; thus \(Q_{n}(T)\) always splits
  and \(z\in K_{n}\) for every \(n\ge 2\).

*  Suppose \(\langle\mu,\nu\rangle\) is finite of order
  \(m\in\{2,3,4,6\}\).  Every element of \(R\setminus\{1\}\) has order
  dividing \(m\).  Consequently
  \(R\setminus\{1\}\) contains an \(n\)-th root of unity
  if and only if \(\gcd(m,n)\neq1\).
  Via \((16)\) we obtain:  

  \[
  Q_{n}(T)\text{ is irreducible over }K_{n}
  \Longleftrightarrow \gcd(m,n)\neq1 .
  \]

  In the splitting case (\(\gcd(m,n)=1\)) the relation
  \(z=\bigl(-B_{n}\pm\sqrt{\Delta_{n}}\bigr)/(2C_{n})\) expresses \(z\)
  as a rational function of \(z^{\,n}\) and \(a,b,c,d\); when
  \(\gcd(m,n)\neq1\) this is impossible because \(z\notin K_{n}\).

%---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
(b)  

Starting from the relation  
\[
x^{2}=u\,x-1, \tag{17}
\]
we repeatedly multiply by \(x\) and reduce every time with \((17)\):

\[
\begin{aligned}
x^{3}&=(u^{2}-1)x-u,\\
x^{4}&=(u^{3}-2u)x-(u^{2}-1),\\
x^{5}&=(u^{4}-3u^{2}+1)x-u(u^{2}-2),\\
x^{6}&=(u^{5}-4u^{3}+3u)x-(u^{4}-3u^{2}+1),\\
x^{7}&=(u^{6}-5u^{4}+6u^{2}-1)x-(u^{5}-4u^{3}+3u). \tag{18}
\end{aligned}
\]
Solving the last line of \((18)\) for \(x\) gives the inversion identity
\[
x=\dfrac{x^{7}+u^{5}-4u^{3}+3u}{u^{6}-5u^{4}+6u^{2}-1},
\]
which coincides with \((9)\).  The denominator is the non-zero
polynomial \(u^{6}-5u^{4}+6u^{2}-1\); hence the formula is valid for all
\(x\) for which this denominator does not vanish.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

•  The original problem dealt with a quadratic and used a single power zⁿ.
   Here we move to a *cubic*, which raises the ambient field degree from 2
   to 3 and forces one to manipulate *three* coefficient–dependent sequences
   simultaneously instead of one.  
•  Part (a) demands an explicit construction of Pₙ,Bₙ,Cₙ, the derivation of a
   pair of coupled linear equations, and the use of Cramer’s rule; none of
   these is needed for the quadratic case.  The proof also has to guarantee
   that some small n works for *all* cubics, invoking a non–trivial argument
   on the coefficients.  
•  Part (b) asks for the recovery of x from (x⁵,u).  The exponent 5 forces
   three rounds of substitutions (compare the single round required when the
   given power is x⁴ in the current kernel variant), and the resulting
   polynomials are of much higher degree (degree 4 in u).  Careful algebra is
   indispensable to avoid sign and coefficient errors.
